question:
An email from 'Netflix Support' says your account is suspended and has a 'Log In' button. 
The sender address is 'netflix-security@gmail.com'. What is the red flag?
options: The use of a Gmail address for official business

question: A friend DMs you: 'OMG, you're in this video!' with a link. What should you do before clicking?
options: Click it to see the video
Ask your friend in person or via a different app if they sent it

question: What is 'Spear Phishing'?
options: Fishing with a spear in the ocean
A generic scam sent to millions of people
A targeted attack using personal info to trick a specific person
A virus that deletes your hard drive

question:
You get an email about a 'Required Password Reset' for your school account. 
The link goes to 'schools-portal.net' instead of '.edu'. This is likely:
options: A typo by the school IT department
A phishing site designed to steal your credentials
A faster way to log in

question:
A pop-up says: 'VIRUS DETECTED! Call 1-800-MY-TECH-HELP now to save your files.' 
What is the safest move?
options: Pay the fee they ask for
Close your browser and restart your computer

question: Why do phishers often create a 'sense of urgency' (e.g., 'Do this in 10 minutes or lose your account!')?
options: To make you act before you have time to think
To help you finish your tasks faster

question:
You see an ad for 'Free V-Bucks' or 'Free Robux' that asks for your login and password.
 What will likely happen?
options: Your account will be hacked and stolen
You get a small amount as a trial

question:
When you hover your mouse over a link in an email, a different web address appears in the corner of your screen.
 Which one is the real destination?
options: The one written in the email
The one shown in the hover-over preview
The one with the most letters

question:
An email claims to be from the government asking for your Social Security Number via email. 
Why is this a scam?
options: Government agencies don't ask for sensitive info over email
The email didn't have a cool logo
It was sent on a Saturday

question: What does the 'S' in HTTPS stand for, and does it guarantee a site is safe?
options: Secure; No, scammers can use HTTPS too

question: Which of these is a common 'Pretexting' scam?
options: A caller pretending to be your grandpa in trouble needing money

question:
You receive an invoice for a $500 purchase you never made.
 It has an attached PDF named 'View_Receipt.exe'. Why is this dangerous?
options: PDFs should not end in '.exe' (which is a program file)
The font on the invoice is ugly

question: What is the best way to check if a 'Security Alert' email from your bank is real?
options: Reply to the email and ask
Call the number listed in the email footer
Log in through the bank's official app or website separately

question:
A scammer uses 'MFA Fatigue' by sending dozens of login approval pings to your phone.
 What should you do?
options: Deny them and change your password immediately
Turn off your phone for a week
Ignore them and hope they go away

question: Which of these looks most like a 'Spoofed' URL?
You get a text saying 'Your FedEx package is delayed. Pay $1 to reschedule.' You aren't expecting a package. What do you do?
options: Enter your card info to see what the package is
Delete the text and block the number

question: What is 'Smishing'?
options: Phishing that happens via SMS (Text Messages)
Phishing that happens on social media
A small version of a computer virus

question:
A website offers you a 'Free iPhone 15' if you just fill out a survey with your address and phone number.
 What is the catch?
options: The survey takes a long time
They will sell your data to scammers and you won't get a phone
The iPhone is a refurbished model
You have to pay for shipping

question: Why is it dangerous to use the same password for your email and your games?
options: If a phisher gets your game password, they can also get into your email

question:
You receive a LinkedIn request from a recruiter at a famous company, 
but their profile has no photo and only 2 connections. This is likely:
What should you do if you realize you accidentally entered your password into a phishing site?
options: Change your password on that site and all others immediately
Wait to see if someone logs in

question: Which of these is a sign of a professional-looking phishing email?
options: A sender address that matches the company name exactly
A link to the actual company homepage

question:
A phisher calls you pretending to be 'Microsoft Tech Support' and asks for remote access to your laptop.
 What should you do?
options: Give them access so they can fix the speed
Ask them for their employee ID first
Let them see just your desktop

question: What is 'Vishing'?
If a website asks you to 'Update your browser' by downloading a file from their page, what should you do?
options: Only update through your computer's official settings or app store
Ask a friend to download it first
Ignore the warning and keep browsing

question: Which part of this email address is the 'Domain'? 'security@amazon.deals.com'

You get an email with a subject line 'RE: Your Application' 
but you never applied for a job. Why did they use 'RE:'?
options: To make it look like a reply to a conversation you already started
To show it is an urgent matter

question: How can Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) stop a phishing attack?
options: It prevents the scammer from seeing the email
Even if they have your password, they still need the code from your phone
It deletes the phishing email automatically
It blocks the scammer's IP address

question: A 'Quishing' attack uses what to lead you to a fake website?
What is the safest way to browse the internet on public Wi-Fi?
options: Don't log into sensitive accounts or use a VPN
